insights: All 500 records show 100% goal completion (250 Closed + 250 Resolved), eliminating variation in completion status and rendering factor analysis of completion rate impossible.

Completion time cannot serve as a reliable performance proxy due to systemic data integrity issues: closed_at precedes opened_at in the majority of cases, evidenced by an overall median completion time of -5.50 days and category-level medians that are uniformly negative.

The completion time distribution is severely distorted — ranging from -355 to +383 days with a standard deviation exceeding 154 days — indicating inconsistent timestamp logic across records and invalidating time-based comparisons across categories or time windows.

No viable performance metric remains for factor analysis: completion rate lacks discriminative power (zero variance), and completion time is compromised by fundamental data capture or processing errors.
Departmental performance cannot be meaningfully benchmarked, segmented, or diagnosed using the current dataset, as both primary outcome dimensions (status and timing) fail basic validity checks.
summary: The core business goal—to analyze factors influencing goal completion rates and their impact on departmental performance—cannot be fulfilled due to fundamental data limitations. All records reflect completed goals (100% completion), eliminating any variation needed to assess drivers of success or failure. Simultaneously, completion time is invalidated by pervasive timestamp errors (e.g., median of -5.50 days), making temporal or categorical comparisons unreliable. As a result, no evidence-based factor analysis is possible, and departmental performance remains unmeasurable with the current data. The immediate priority must be diagnosing and correcting the root cause of timestamp misalignment and verifying whether incomplete goals exist outside the sampled records.
